claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age.**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children you'd like to check, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and wanted to check if I'm eligible.

I have one child - my son Alex. He's 6 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Wonderful, thank you for that information! Since Alex is 6 years old, he falls into the **under 16** category, which is great news for eligibility purposes.

Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

At 6 years old, I would assume Alex isn't in paid employment, but I need to confirm this for the assessment. Is that correct - Alex is not working, or works fewer than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't work at all. He's only 6, so he's just at primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, this means Alex is not personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name.

Given his age, I would expect this to be the case, but can you confirm that Alex is not receiving any such benefits in his own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a young child, so nothing like that applies to him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected. Let me continue with the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

At 6 years old and in primary school, I'm confident the answer is no, but I just need to confirm - Alex is **not** currently in any kind of apprenticeship programme, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - he's far too young for an apprenticeship! He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly, of course! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great progress! Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a UK resident living in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can financially support yourself and your family
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to your situation? For most people, it's simply that they **normally live in the UK**.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. That's an important detail.

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant?**

Crown servants include people such as:
- UK government employees working overseas (e.g., diplomats, civil servants posted abroad)
- Members of the Armed Forces stationed overseas

If you're not a Crown servant, unfortunately you would generally need to be living in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit.

Does the Crown servant situation apply to you, or are you living abroad for other reasons?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant. I'm just living abroad for personal reasons.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, I've reached a conclusion about your eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Assessment Result: **Not Eligible**

I'm afraid you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

### Why?

While Alex meets all the requirements related to the child (he's under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship), **you do not meet the UK residency requirements** because you are living abroad for personal reasons.

### Residency Requirements

To claim Child Benefit, you must:
- **Normally live in the UK**, OR
- Have **settled or pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme (with certain conditions), OR
- Be a **Crown servant** living abroad

### What You Can Do

1. **If you return to the UK** - You may become eligible once you're normally resident in the UK again. You could then apply for Child Benefit at that time.

2. **Check for other support** - If you're a UK citizen living abroad, there may be other support or tax credits available depending on which country you're living in and your circumstances.

3. **Get further advice** - If your situation is more complex (e.g., you work partly in the UK, or your circumstances may change), you might want to contact HMRC directly for personalised guidance.

I'm sorry this wasn't the outcome you were hoping for. Is there anything else I can help you with?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), is not working, is not receiving benefits in his own right, and is not in an apprenticeship - all of which would normally support eligibility. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. They are living abroad for personal reasons and are not a Crown servant. To claim Child Benefit, the claimant must normally live in the UK, have settled or pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, or be a Crown servant living abroad.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. While their child Alex meets all the child-related criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship), the claimant themselves must normally live in the UK or meet specific circumstances such as having settled/pre-settled status or being a Crown servant abroad.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

To claim Child Benefit, the claimant must normally live in the UK, have settled or pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, or be a Crown servant living abroad.

While their child Alex meets all the child-related criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship), the claimant themselves must normally live in the UK or meet specific circumstances such as having settled/pre-settled status or being a Crown servant abroad.
